title: Model-agnostic cooling algorithms for strongly interacting fermions
authors: Henning Schlömer, Liyuan Chen, Susanne F. Yelin, Hong-Ye Hu
abstract: Strongly interacting fermions underpin some of the most challenging problems in condensed matter physics, such as high-temperature superconductivity. The low-energy states of these systems encode their essential microscopic properties, yet remain largely inaccessible to classical methods. Quantum simulation offers a promising path forward, and among state-preparation strategies, engineered dissipation has emerged as a particularly compelling approach. Existing cooling protocols, however, typically rely on knowledge of the quasiparticle spectrum or mappings to free-fermion limits. In this letter, we introduce a randomized, symmetry-preserving cooling algorithm that requires no spectral information, using only local coupling operators to ancilla degrees of freedom with randomly sampled energy splittings to drive generic fermionic systems toward their low-energy manifold. We benchmark the protocol on canonical correlated fermionic models relevant to high-temperature superconductors, spanning metallic, density-wave, paired, superconducting, and phase-separated phases. Across all models, we observe universal cooling behavior: monotonic energy relaxation, concentration of spectral weight at low energies, and stabilization of correlated ground-state order. Our results establish randomized dissipative cooling as a general strategy for preparing strongly correlated fermionic states on programmable quantum devices.
summary: This paper introduces a new cooling algorithm that uses randomized dissipation to prepare the ground states of strongly interacting fermions. Unlike previous methods, it does not require knowledge of the system's energy spectrum, making it highly versatile for complex models like the t-J model. The protocol is shown to be robust against noise and effective at stabilizing various correlated phases.
main_problem: Preparing the low-energy ground states of strongly interacting fermionic systems is difficult because classical methods suffer from the sign problem and existing quantum protocols require prior knowledge of the system's energy spectrum.
main_result: The authors demonstrate a randomized, model-agnostic dissipative cooling algorithm that achieves monotonic energy relaxation and stabilizes correlated ground-state order without needing spectral information.
method: A randomized dissipative protocol where the system is coupled to ancilla degrees of freedom via local, symmetry-preserving operators and randomly sampled energy splittings, followed by ancilla reset cycles.
model_or_system: The study focuses on strongly interacting fermionic lattice models, including the 1D spinless t-t'-V chain, the t-J model, and a 2D spinless fermion lattice, all relevant to high-temperature superconductors.
key_observables: Normalized energy density, ground-state fidelity, local densities, spin-spin and pair-pair correlations, and the relaxation rate.
important_parameters: Coupling strength g, interaction time tau, ancilla frequency range [omega_min, omega_max], interaction strengths (t, t', V, J), and dephasing probability p.
main_assumptions: The protocol relies on the Rotating Wave Approximation (RWA) for the system-ancilla coupling and assumes the Eigenstate Thermalization Hypothesis (ETH) to ensure sufficient spectral connectivity.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the cooling algorithm schematic and coupling toolbox; Figure 5 compares stability against dephasing and different ancilla architectures; Figure 6 compares directed vs. random hopping sequences.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of state preparation, describes the randomized dissipative algorithm and its coupling toolbox, presents benchmarks on various correlated fermionic models, analyzes scalability and noise robustness, and concludes with limitations and open questions.
why_interesting: It presents a general-purpose, model-agnostic strategy for state preparation on programmable quantum devices, specifically addressing the challenge of cooling systems where the energy spectrum is unknown.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Cooling algorithm for strongly interacting fermions. Schematic illustration of the algorithm using ei- ther a single ancilla or a bulk ancilla architecture to me- diate energy-selective interactions with the system. A cou- pling toolbox enables cooling within a chosen symmetry sec- tor. Energy-selective interactions between system and ancillas with randomly chosen energy splittings transfer population from higher to lower many-body energy levels.

FIG. 2. Cooling dynamics in the spinless t-t′-V chain. (a) Normalized energy density ε(n), Eq. (4), vs. cooling cycle n for t/t′ = 2, g/t = 0.025, τt = 40, V/t = 0, 1, 4, L = 10 sites and Np = 5. The initial state has all fermions local- ized on the left side of the chain. Inset: steady state weights wk of the lowest eigenstates k. (b) Local densities ⟨ni⟩in the cooled steady state (solid lines) compared with the ex- act ground state (crosses). (c) Matrix elements |Akk′|2 in the energy eigenbasis for V/t = 0 (upper part) and V/t = 4 (lower part), restricted to the lowest 100 eigenstates. Arrows indicate the many-body bandwidth ∆E = E99 −E0 and the cutoff ωmax corresponding to optimal...

FIG. 3. Cooling doped Mott insulators. (a) 1D t-J chain across metallic, paired, and phase-separated regimes as J/t is tuned. Charge and spin coupling channels are combined (see main text); L = 10, Np = 4, g = 0.05, τt = 10, ω/t ∈[0, 3], and total spin sector ˆSz tot = 0. Shown are normalized energy relaxation for J/t = 0.5, 2, 3 and corresponding on-site densities of the steady state (solid lines) and the ground state (black crosses). The initial state is a N´eel product state with particles sitting at the boundary. (b) Mixed-dimensional t∥-J∥-J⊥ladder for a 4 × 2 system with Np = 4, J⊥/t∥= 2, J∥/t∥= 1. Cooling is implemented via charge hopping ˆAc ∥with fixed ω/t∥= 0.8, starting in an...

FIG. 4. Spinless fermions in 2D. Cooling dynamics (a) and steady state decomposition (b) of interacting spinless fermions on a 3 × 3 lattice with Np = 5, g/t = 0.05, τt = 10, and ωmax/t = 2, 6, 8 for V/t = 0.1, 1, 4, respectively. The coupling operator is chosen as a directed Z-shaped path in the 2D lattice, shown in the inset of panel (a).

FIG. 5. Noise robustness and different architectures. (a) Robustness against global dephasing in the 1D t-t′-V model. Shown are the normalized energy ϵ and the fideli- ties with respect to the ground state and to the low energy subspace spanned by the ten lowest eigenstates, evaluated af- ter 1000 cooling cycles. Parameters are identical to Fig. 2 with V/t = 1. (b) Comparison of different system-ancilla ar- chitectures for a 1D chain with V/t = 4, L = 6, and Np = 3: single global ancilla, one ancilla per bond, and boundary-only cooling.
